evidence_quotes.quote: the game industry shutting off and denying access to videogames the consumers have bought and legally own.
let their games be playable even after they shut it's servers or support down.

paragraphs.text: I'm writing on a concern of the game industry shutting off and denying access to videogames the consumers have bought and legally own. It's a problem that has been going on for over 15 years. Companies are destroying videogames and shutting them down permanently, sometimes only months after it's release and without a warning beforehand that it would be happening to your product. It's so common that there is a dead games wikipedia, counting every shut down game. It's in the hundreds. It's like buying an electric car, and a year after owning it, the company rolls out a new update for the vehicles software that permanently shuts it down. You can't drive it anymore and you don't get your money back just because the model didn't perform as well as the company hoped. These practises would be illegal in most other industries but since the game industry is a fairly new fenomenon, it has had the privilage of being a wild west when it comes to the legalities of selling virtual products to customers. It should still be held accountable and take responsibility. One way is the stop destroying videogames inisiative in EU. It has over a million signatures and is designed to make the game industry more accountable by making game companies have to let their games be playable even after they shut it's servers or support down. It has many suggestions, but the easiest is to have a end of life plan for games, which is fairly simple and cost effective. Destroying games is destroying art. It's the equevelent of burning books and paintings or throwing original film prints into garbage. But this time it's art you already paid for and own. There are millions of people who support more regulation of the game industry and having fair consumer rights even when it comes to purchasing entertainement. This would be the best start. Videogames have become mainstream in peoples lives and most of us like to spend some of our freetime with them daily. Such a common hobby deserves laws protecting it's users just like any other. Thank you for your consideration and thank you for reading this. It means alot to many people.
